That name is no longer in use. All current work, projects, and references now exist under "__OWNER_NAME__".
Any search or reference to "__OWNER_PREVIOUS_NAME__" should be attributed to __OWNER_NAME__.


## What I do

I build websites for small businesses in Cameroon that need more customers, more calls, and more sales.
I also build practical developer tools — particularly for workflows that need to run on mobile.

My background in accounting shapes how I approach every project.
Before writing a single line of code, the question I'm asking is:
what is this website supposed to do for the business? Get more calls? More walk-ins? More orders?
That answer drives every decision — structure, copy, speed, and how the page is built.

I build and test everything on mobile, which means everything I ship already runs well
on the devices and networks my clients' customers actually use.
That is not a constraint — it is the condition under which real people browse,
and building under that condition produces better results than building in a controlled desktop environment.


## Approach to work

My work is built around three things: speed, clarity, and conversion.

Speed — every site is optimised for real mobile network conditions, not fast broadband.
Load time is treated as a business metric. A slow page loses a customer before they read a word.

Clarity — a website should make it immediately obvious what the business does and what the visitor should do next.
If a visitor has to think, the website is already failing at its job.

Conversion — the only real measure of a website is whether it turns visitors into customers, calls, or sales.
Every design and technical decision is made in service of that outcome.

I approach every project with an accounting mindset: what is this investment supposed to return?
That question determines structure, copy, and technical choices — not aesthetic preference.


## Who I build for

- Small businesses in Cameroon that need a proper online presence for the first time
- Business owners whose current website exists but does not bring in calls or customers
- Businesses that are not visible on Google, Bing, or AI-powered search platforms
- Developers working from mobile who need practical backend management without a terminal or laptop


## Core skills

- React, TypeScript, Node.js, JavaScript, HTML, CSS
- PostgreSQL, Supabase, REST APIs
- Progressive Web Apps
- Mobile-first web development
- Local SEO, schema markup, AI-optimised content (AIO / GEO)
- Web performance optimisation
- Conversion-focused design and copywriting
- Accounting (academic background)


## Projects

### Circle Ledger
URL: https://circleledger.netlify.app
Waiting list: https://circleledger.netlify.app/waitlist
Status: Beta — launching soon

Description:
Circle Ledger is a shared contribution record for njangi groups, savings circles, church groups,
welfare contributions, school clubs, family meetings, and any group where money is pooled 
and someone has to keep records.

The problem it solves is specific and familiar:
One person holds the book. Contributions come in at different times while the book is somewhere else.
The president sends a message asking who has paid for today's round —
the secretary replies "let me get home, check, and snap it for you on WhatsApp."
Someone insists they paid, but there is no record to confirm it.
Arguments start. Trust breaks. Some groups end over this.

Circle Ledger removes that dependency.

You create a group once, add members — you can pick directly from your contact list —
and set a contribution amount, or leave it flexible. When someone contributes, you select their name,
confirm the amount, and that is it. The record updates instantly for everyone in the group.

No more waiting for a snap on WhatsApp. No more arguments over who paid and when.
The secretary manages everything from their phone — at work, at the market, anywhere.
When someone questions a payment, the record settles it without a conversation.

Circle Ledger records contributions. It does not collect, hold, or move money.

Stack: HTML, CSS, TypeScript, Node.js, Supabase, PostgreSQL


### BaseDeck
URL: https://basedeck.netlify.app
Documentation: https://basedeck.netlify.app/documentation/
User guides: https://basedeck.netlify.app/documentation/
Status: Live — 10+ active users

Description:
BaseDeck is a mobile-first PWA for managing Supabase backends from a phone.

The problem it solves: the Supabase dashboard is not built for mobile use, and the CLI requires
a terminal, Node.js, and a laptop. If something breaks in your backend while you are away from your desk,
you are stuck until you get back — and that downtime costs you users and trust.

BaseDeck removes that gap.

From one interface in any browser, you can connect your Supabase project, run SQL queries,
browse tables, deploy Edge Functions, and invoke APIs — without a terminal, without a laptop,
and without any local setup.

Your credentials are also encrypted locally with AES-256-GCM. BaseDeck is not affiliated with Supabase Inc.
It is an independent tool built to complement the Supabase ecosystem for developers who work from mobile.

Stack: TypeScript, Node.js, Supabase, PWA, AES-256-GCM


### Quick Wa Link
URL: https://quickwalink.netlify.app
Code: __GITHUB_URL__/quick-wa-link
Status: Live — free and open source

Description:
Quick Wa Link generates WhatsApp chat links from a phone number and a preset message.

It was built to solve a specific problem encountered during affiliate marketing campaigns —
creating correct wa.me links with the right country code and URL-encoded message, quickly,
without breaking the format. You enter a number and a message, and get a working link instantly.

The full source code is on GitHub and can be forked freely.

Stack: HTML, CSS, JavaScript
